danger_signals.visual_form: Animated creature sprites (armadillos rolling, spitting flowers, jumping plants, chomping entities)
danger_signals.meaning: Direct threat to player character; collision results in damage or death

danger_signals.visual_form: Sawblades, logs, cannons positioned throughout levels
danger_signals.meaning: Stationary and dynamic hazards that block progress or cause harm

danger_signals.visual_form: Unique enemy variants (e.g., Rufus Ruffcut boss arena with sawblades and logs)
danger_signals.meaning: Escalated danger zone; significant obstacle requiring skill to overcome

safety_signals.meaning: Safe routes through levels allow player advancement
Familiar franchise character establishes tone of lighthearted challenge

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Cartoon character sprites from original series
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Level theming reflects cartoon source material aesthetics

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Varied enemy sprite designs (armadillos, snakes, fish, crabs, etc.)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple creature types suggest diverse threat ecosystem rather than unified faction
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Level-specific enemy placement creates themed obstacle zones

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Japanese release (Chiki Chiki Machine Mou Race)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blue title screen background; 5:00 minute timer
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Longer time limit provides different difficulty curve

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Red title screen background; rebranded title as 'Wacky Races'
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Reduced timer to 3:00 increases difficulty; regional localization affects visual identity

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy sprite variety increases across stages; hazard density escalates
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies exhibit more complex movement patterns (jumping, rolling, flying) as player advances

environmental_storytelling.example: Water-themed level B features fish, hermit crabs, lobsters, octopuses; creates aquatic danger ecosystem through visual design
Sawblades and logs positioned to force specific platforming sequences; communicates level design intent through spatial arrangement
Rufus Ruffcut boss fight uses thematic sawblades and logs, signaling a unique challenge distinct from standard level hazards
Character and creature sprites directly reference original Wacky Races cartoon; visual consistency maintains franchise identity and communicates 'known world' to players familiar with source material

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Constantly rolling; always in motion suggests aggressive, relentless pursuit
Animated sprite with mouth open; projectile attacks signal ranged threat
Walks back and forth constantly; territorial behavior indicates localized hazard
Constantly flying; aerial movement suggests unpredictable threat angle
Jumping animation communicates vertical threat; chomping action signals bite hazard
Horizontal movement pattern; rapid movement suggests evasive threat
Constantly on move; persistent movement suggests momentum-based hazard

notes: Wacky Races NES is a platformer adaptation of a racing cartoon. Visual communication relies heavily on sprite-based enemy variety and movement patterns rather than HUD elements or explicit world-state indicators. Regional differences (blue vs. red title screens, timer adjustments) suggest adaptive design for different markets rather than narrative world-state shifts. The game emphasizes hazard telegraphing through character animation and sprite design; unused enemy graphics indicate development iteration on threat variety. No explicit faction visual language exists—the game communicates through cartoon IP recognition and enemy type differentiation. Behavioral manifestation occurs primarily through animation (rolling, jumping, flying, walking patterns) rather than state-based visual changes.
